title: Friendship & Cooperation
objective: Students will explore friendship & cooperation concepts including Vocabulary: working with others, Listening to Others, Makes someone a good friend and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Vocabulary: working with others? Let's find out together!

Know and use the vocabulary of working with others — cooperate, share, take turns, team, listen, agree, disagree respectfully, and include — and understand that these words describe habits that friendships and group work depend on
Time to try this out: Use 'cooperate' and 'compromise' correctly — e.g. 'We cooperated by taking turns choosing the game'
When you is working on something with a friend or sibling, can they use words like 'cooperate' or 'take turns' to describe what they're doing — and actually practise those things?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Vocabulary: working with others: Use 'cooperate' and 'compromise' correctly — e.g. 'We cooperated by taking turns choosing the game' Explain what 'active listening' means and demonstrate it: facing the speaker, not interrupting, responding to what was said
Today we're going to learn about Listening to Others. Get ready for an adventure!
Listen to others when they are speaking — looking at the speaker, waiting until they finish, and showing they have heard by responding to what was said rather than just talking about their own ideas
Let's practice together! Face the speaker and maintain attention while someone else is talking
When a friend is telling you about their weekend, does you actually listen and ask questions about it — rather than immediately jumping in to talk about their own weekend?
Great job! Today we learned about Listening to Others. Face the speaker and maintain attention while someone else is talking Wait until the other person has finished before responding
Let's explore Makes someone a good friend! Have you ever wondered about this?

Understand what makes someone a good friend — being kind, honest, reliable, and including others — and recognise behaviours that are not friendly, such as being bossy, leaving people out, or saying mean things
Let's practice together! List at least four qualities of a good friend
Can you describe what makes a good friend and recognise when someone — including themselves — isn't being a good friend?
Great job! Today we learned about Makes someone a good friend. List at least four qualities of a good friend Identify unfriendly behaviour in a story or real situation
Have you ever thought about Taking Turns and Sharing? Let's find out together!

Take turns, share materials, and play cooperatively with others — understanding that group activities work better when everyone gets a fair go and that waiting for your turn is part of being a good friend
Time to try this out: Take turns in a game or group activity without needing adult reminders
When you is playing a board game with friends or siblings, can they wait for their turn and share the pieces without grabbing or getting upset?
Great job! Today we learned about Taking Turns and Sharing. Take turns in a game or group activity without needing adult reminders Share materials willingly during a shared task or playtime
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Asking for Help!
Ask for help when they need it — from a friend, teacher, or family member — and understand that asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness
Let me show you something cool. Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly
If you is stuck on something at school or feeling worried about something, will they ask a teacher or friend for help rather than struggling in silence?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Asking for Help: Approach an appropriate person and ask for help clearly Identify who to go to for different kinds of help (academic, emotional, practical)
